WA3857 Lab Guide

GitHub Actions and CI/CD Pipelines for Leaders

Add a CI Check to the Evergreen Quote Repository

Lab 1

1.1. Why This Lab Exists

In an earlier lab in this program you took the Evergreen quote page from a folder on your VM to a real GitHub repository, opened a pull request, self-reviewed the diff, and squash-merged it into main. That ritual — branch, commit, push, open PR, review, merge — is half of the modern code-change story.

The other half is what happens automatically on every pull request: tests, linting, a build, a security scan. Those checks run in GitHub Actions, and when someone on your team says "the build is red" or "CI is broken" they are describing what you are about to practice reading.

You are not going to author a pipeline from scratch. You are going to drop in a tiny, hand-built workflow file so the next pull request you open shows a live Actions run in the GitHub UI. Then you will read that run the way a leader reads a dashboard: did it pass, how long did it take, and if it failed, what does the log say?

By the end of this lab you will have:

  • A GitHub Actions workflow file committed to your Evergreen repo.

  • One pull request whose Checks tab shows a completed CI run.

  • A green check mark next to your latest commit on main.

  • Confidence reading an Actions run summary and log output.

 Tip

As with the earlier lab, the pedagogical win is the workflow, not the workflow file. The five lines of YAML you paste in are a placeholder for "any CI pipeline." The ritual — add a workflow, open a PR, watch the Checks tab light up, read the log, merge — is the sequence to recognize.

1.2. Prerequisite: Complete the Evergreen Quote Pull Request Lab

This lab continues directly from the Evergreen Quote Pull Request lab. Before starting here, you should already have:

  • An evergreen-quote-demo repository on github.com under your provided GitHub account, with the Evergreen files pushed to main.

  • That repository on your lab VM at ~/Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request.

  • Your local main branch up to date with the squash-merged change from the previous lab.

If any of those are not true, stop and complete the Evergreen Quote Pull Request lab first. The starter files for that lab live in Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request on your VM.

1.3. Open the Repository in VS Code

  1. Launch VS Code from the lab VM’s applications menu.

  2. In VS Code, choose File → Open Folder, navigate to Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request in your home folder, and click Open. (This is the same folder you used in the earlier lab — the CI workflow will be added to the same project.)

  3. Open the VS Code integrated terminal via Terminal → New Terminal (or press Ctrl+` ). Confirm you are on main and up to date:

    git status
git checkout main
git pull origin main

    You should see On branch main and Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'. If git status shows you are on a different branch from a previous lab, the git checkout main line switches you back before the pull.

1.4. Create a Feature Branch

  1. In the VS Code terminal, create and switch to a feature branch so the workflow file arrives through a pull request rather than directly on main:

    git checkout -b add-ci-check

    The branch name — add-ci-check — describes the change. Same naming habit as the earlier lab.

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm the branch:

    git branch --show-current

    You see add-ci-check.

1.5. Add the Workflow File

GitHub Actions reads any YAML file under .github/workflows/ in your repository. The folder name matters exactly — lowercase, and the dot at the start is not a typo.

  1. In VS Code’s Explorer pane, right-click the repository root and choose New Folder. Name it .github.

  2. Right-click the new .github folder and choose New Folder again. Name this one workflows.

  3. Right-click the workflows folder and choose New File. Name it ci.yml.

  4. Paste the following contents into ci.yml and save:

    name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  check:
    name: Syntax check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out the code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - name: Verify app.js parses
        run: node --check app.js

    Read the file top to bottom before moving on. You should be able to say in plain language what each block does:

    • name: — the label you’ll see in the Actions tab.

    • on: — when the workflow runs (here, on any PR targeting main).

    • jobs: — what runs. We have one job named check.

    • runs-on: — what kind of machine runs it. ubuntu-latest is GitHub’s hosted Linux runner.

    • steps: — the ordered commands inside the job. Each step either uses a prebuilt action (uses:) or runs a shell command (run:).

 Tip

If YAML indentation feels finicky, that’s normal. The rule is two spaces, no tabs, and every child item must be indented further than its parent. VS Code will highlight a YAML error with a red squiggle if you get it wrong.

1.6. Commit and Push the Feature Branch

  1. In the VS Code terminal, stage and commit:

    git add .github/workflows/ci.yml
git status
git commit -m "Add minimal CI workflow"

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to push the new branch to GitHub. The first push of a new branch needs the -u flag to set the upstream:

    git push -u origin add-ci-check

    GitHub confirms the push and often prints a URL you can click to open the compare-and-pull-request page directly. You can either click that URL or open the repo in your browser.

  3. In your browser, navigate to your evergreen-quote-demo repository on github.com — the same repo URL you used in the earlier lab (https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/evergreen-quote-demo).

  4. A yellow banner and a green button appear at the top: "add-ci-check had recent pushes — Compare & pull request."

     Note

    If you don’t see the yellow banner, it has either timed out or you missed it on first load. No problem — click the Pull requests tab near the top of the repo page, then click the green New pull request button. On the compare page, set base: main ← compare: add-ci-check using the two dropdowns. This lands you on the same form the yellow banner shortcut would have opened.

1.7. Open the Pull Request

  1. Click Compare & pull request (or Create pull request if you took the Pull requests tab route). You land on the Open-a-pull-request form. The base dropdown should say main; the compare dropdown should say add-ci-check. Confirm both.

  2. For the title, enter:

    Add minimal CI workflow

  3. For the body, write a real PR description — same Summary / Why / Test plan template you used in the earlier lab:

    ## Summary

Adds a minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs on every pull
request targeting `main`.

## Why

We want a visible pass/fail signal on pull requests. This is the
smallest workflow that demonstrates the Actions feedback loop; a
real pipeline will replace the syntax check with a test suite later.

## Test plan

- The Checks tab on this PR shows a completed "CI" workflow.
- The "Verify app.js parses" step finishes with a green check.
- No application files (`index.html`, `styles.css`, `app.js`) are
  modified by this PR.

  4. Scroll down on the form. The Files changed section previews your diff. The only file in the diff should be .github/workflows/ci.yml — green additions, no red deletions.

  5. Click Create pull request.

1.8. Watch the Run

  1. As soon as the PR is created, scroll down on the PR page. You see a Checks section. Within a few seconds, a CI / Syntax check entry appears with a yellow "in progress" dot.

  2. Click Details next to that entry (or click the Actions tab at the top of the repository). You land in the run page — one of the most important screens in your engineering life. By default this drops you on the selected job (Syntax check); the left sidebar shows Summary, All jobs, and Run details.

  3. Click Summary in the left sidebar. Take a moment to absorb the layout:

    • The run summary page (the one you just opened) shows the run-level header: who triggered it, the commit SHA, the branch, the event (pull_request), and the total duration. A jobs-overview graph and any artifacts the run produced also live here.

    • The jobs list in the left sidebar (we only have one job, Syntax check). Click it to switch from the run summary view to the per-job view.

  4. In the left sidebar, click Syntax check under All jobs to switch from the run summary view to the per-job view. The step log appears on the right.

    • The step log on the right of the job view — every step from ci.yml appears here as an expandable row.

1.9. Read a Failure

It is worth seeing a red run on purpose before you merge a green one. Doing this once makes the Actions tab much less mysterious.

  1. Back in VS Code, still on the add-ci-check branch, open app.js and delete a closing curly brace } somewhere near the bottom. Save.

  2. In the VS Code terminal, commit and push:

    git add app.js
git commit -m "Intentional break to see a failing run"
git push

  3. Switch back to the PR page in your browser (use the Pull requests tab and click your open PR, or click Back to pull request in the top-left if you’re still on a run page). Scroll down to the Checks section. Within a few seconds, a new CI / Syntax check entry appears — the in-progress yellow dot turns into a red X once the run finishes.

  4. Click View Details next to the failed CI / Syntax check entry. You land on the run page for this new run (a different run from the green one you read earlier — confirm by checking the run title / commit message at the top: it should match Intentional break to see a failing run).

  5. In the left sidebar under All jobs, click Syntax check. In the step log on the right, find the Verify app.js parses step — it has a red X next to it — and click to expand it. The log shows a Node syntax error pointing at the line you broke. That is the same kind of output your engineering teams read when they say "CI is broken."

  1. Switch back to the VS Code terminal. You are still on the add-ci-check branch with the broken app.js. You need to undo that change before you can merge the PR.

  2. Undo the change so the PR can merge:

    git revert --no-edit HEAD
git push

    Wait for the new run to turn green before continuing.

1.10. Self-Review and Merge

  1. On the PR page, click Files changed. Confirm the only change that will land is your .github/workflows/ci.yml file.

  2. Hover over the new line name: CI in the diff. A blue + icon appears in the gutter. Click it and leave a short inline comment:

    Reviewed. Single new workflow file, no application changes.

    Submit it with Start a review, then click Submit review in the top-right.

  3. Three radio buttons appear: Comment, Approve, Request changes. As you saw in the earlier lab, Approve is greyed out with "Pull request authors can’t approve their own pull requests." Select Comment and click Submit review.

  1. Scroll down to the merge controls. Click the dropdown arrow next to the green Merge pull request button and select Squash and merge — same default you used in the earlier lab. Confirm the merge and click Delete branch when GitHub offers.

1.11. Pull the Merge Back Down

  1. Back in the VS Code terminal, switch to main and pull the latest:

    git checkout main
git pull origin main

    You now have the workflow file on main. Any future pull request against this repository will automatically get a CI run attached to it.

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm:

    git log --oneline

    You see the squash commit from the earlier lab’s hero-heading change, plus a new squash commit "Add minimal CI workflow" on top.

  3. Your local feature branch still exists. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to clean it up:

    git branch -d add-ci-check

1.12. What You Just Did

You just completed the second half of the code-change ritual, end-to-end:

  1. Branched off main for a CI-only change.

  2. Authored a GitHub Actions workflow file from scratch.

  3. Pushed the branch and opened a pull request.

  4. Watched a live CI run appear in the Checks tab.

  5. Read the run page — summary, jobs list, step log.

  6. (Optionally) saw a red run, read the failure output, and recovered with git revert.

  7. Self-reviewed the diff, left an inline comment, and submitted a Comment review.

  8. Squash-merged the PR into main and deleted the branch.

  9. Pulled the merge back down to your local main.

 Important

Every pull request on any active repository now triggers the same pattern you just watched. The engineers you partner with read the Checks tab dozens of times a day. You do not need to author pipelines — but you need to recognize a green check, a red X, and an in-progress yellow dot, and you need to know that clicking Details takes you to the log that explains why. You now do.

1.13. Review

Take a moment to open the Actions tab at the top of the repo. You see a list with at least one entry per PR run. Each entry shows the commit, who pushed, how long it took, and whether it passed. That list is the single best page to open when someone tells you "the build is broken": newest at the top, red ones jump out, and clicking any row opens the same run page you read earlier.

 Tip

Make it a habit to glance at the Actions tab on one repo you care about every day for the next two weeks. Pair it with the "one-PR-a-day" habit from the earlier lab — read the PR description, then read its CI run. Two minutes of reading buys you ten minutes of context the next time that codebase comes up in a meeting.
